chapters.title: How generalization became a path to success
chapters.text:
One of the best examples of early expertise is professional golf player Tiger Woods. His father, Earl Woods, started training him as soon as he could walk at just ten months old. Tiger engaged in “deliberate practice,” practicing golf most of the time. At age two, Tiger entered his first tournament and won the ten-and-under division. By the time he got to college, he was already world-famous for his exceptional sportsmanship. Today, he ranks among the greatest golfers of all time.

Tiger has come to symbolize the myth that is predominant in our society today — that success hinges on the quantity and early commencement of deliberate practice.

We often assume that specialization provides a competitive advantage.

Contrast this with Roger Federer, whose upbringing defied the Tiger model. Even though his mother was a tennis coach, she never forced him to play the game. Federer grew up playing various sports and only started focusing on tennis in his teenage years. But this didn’t hamper his development in the long run. Today, he is widely deemed one of the greatest tennis players ever.

While society glorifies the stories of athletes following the Tiger path, the Roger path to stardom is more common. In-depth analyses of athletes' development trajectories from early childhood reveal that future champions don't initially devote most of their time to intense training.

Instead, they undergo a “sampling phase,” exploring different sports to acquire various skills before focusing on one.

While there is no doubt that some fields require people with Tiger's clarity and early skill, the world also needs more Rogers as complexity increases. We need people with range — individuals who start broad and embrace diverse experiences and perspectives as they progress.

chapters.title: Specialization is not a good idea where patterns rarely recur
chapters.text:
Do specialists really get better with experience? Psychologists Daniel Kahneman and Gary Klein independently conducted several studies to answer this question. They both found out that there is no substantial correlation between experience and expertise.

According to Kahneman and Klein, the main factor determining whether experience will inevitably lead to expertise depends on the domain. For example, while specific experience benefits some fields, like chess or firefighting, it falls short for financial or political analysts.

Other studies have also proven that experience and repetition simply did not improve performance or learning in a wide range of real-world scenarios. This occurrence is especially true for those domains which involve human behavior and where there is no apparent repetition of patterns.

There are two kinds of learning environments: the “kind” and “wicked.”

In “kind” domains, patterns are clearly and continuously repeated, information is readily available, and feedback is immediate and accurate, as seen in golf and chess. In this environment, a learner improves simply by practicing the same activity repeatedly with the willingness to do better.

In contrast, “wicked” domains lack the necessary information, guidelines are unclear, and situations are dynamic and influenced by others. Even if there are repetitive patterns in the wicked domain, they are often not obvious. Additionally, feedback is often delayed and inaccurate.

In the most devilishly wicked learning environments, experience will reinforce the exact wrong lessons. ~ David Epstein

Most life pursuits fall into the “wicked” category. After all, the world is not some sport with constrained guidelines and repetitive patterns. No one is going to give you the rules. It's entirely up to you to figure out how things work; they can change at any time without notice. So, early specialization and a narrow focus are not conducive to success in this complex and ever-changing world.

chapters.title: Having range versus specializing in the contemporary world
chapters.text:
A Hungarian man, Laszlo Polgar, took the Tiger approach with his daughters. Before the children were even born, Lazlo made it his life's goal to mold them into chess grandmasters through intensive focus and deliberate practice. He gathered an enormous database of chess games and home-schooled his children. He taught his girls how to play from a very tender age and made them follow a strict practice schedule.

Lazlo's experiment worked. It worked so well that the Polgar sisters are still highly revered today for their tactical genius in chess.

The popular book, “Talent Is Overrated,” cites the Polgar sisters and Tiger Woods' journey to argue that targeted practice and early initiation are crucial for success in any field.

The problem with this reasoning is that no approach is omnicompetent — not everything can be mastered using the same strategy.

The tales of the Polgar sisters and Tiger Woods can mislead people into thinking that expertise always emerges from a very conducive learning environment. But, if that were true, early and specialized focus would be typically effective. However, it's not the case; this approach usually doesn't work, especially in sports.

If exceptional performance relied on extensive and specialized training in a specific field from an early age, child prodigies would invariably become prominent adults. According to psychologist Ellen Winner, an expert on gifted children, child prodigies rarely become renowned “innovators” who revolutionize their fields.

While intensive, focused practice is effective in specific spheres like surgery, poker, bridge, and accounting, even slight rule changes can make experts seem like novices. Psychologist Erik Dane terms this phenomenon “cognitive entrenchment.” And his solution to the problem is simple: “have a foot outside your world.”

Creative achievers don't fixate narrowly; they embrace broad interests.

Successful adapters have range. They excel at evading cognitive fixation and can transpose knowledge innovatively across domains. Their expertise lies in avoiding outdated methodologies. Their approach disrupts their tendencies toward antiquated solutions by leveraging external experiences and metaphors.

Indeed, having range is a strategic advantage in an unpredictable world with few inflexible rules and ambiguous challenges.

chapters.title: Knowing when to quit for success
chapters.text:
David Epstein shares the story of Dutch painter Vincent van Gogh. As a young man, Van Gogh dabbled in various careers and always emerged unsuccessful. He enrolled in an art school when he was 33, only to drop out almost as soon as he got in.

Van Gogh aspired to create art that resonated with the average person, not elitist pieces for art connoisseurs. Through exploration and trial and error, he developed a distinctive style. Now, Van Gogh stands as a pivotal figure in the annals of Western art. Most of his most renowned works were completed in the final two years of his life before he died at 37.

Economists refer to the alignment between an individual’s work, innate inclinations, and skills as “match quality.” A study by Northwestern University economist Ofer Malamud showed that students with more sampling opportunities have a higher match quality later in life than students forced to follow a career path.

People with high match quality are unafraid to switch careers anytime.

In our society today, we are often barraged by advice like “winners never quit and quitters never win.” While this saying may be well-intentioned, it is lousy advice. Author Seth Godin asserts that “winners” — individuals who excel in their fields — are not hesitant to abandon a path when it is unsuitable. They quit fast and don't feel bad about it.

Continuing on a career path that doesn't bring us happiness simply because we don't have the guts to quit and try something new is a recipe for disaster. Knowing when to quit and pivot to a different plan is a strategic advantage.

Persistence and zeal aren't trivial; a single setback shouldn't indicate giving up. Know that no rule says you must follow the Tiger path. Shifting interests or refocusing goals isn't a flaw; it's a competitive edge.

Did you know? The stories of French artist Paul Gauguin and British author J. K. Rowling parallel Van Gogh's. Starting their careers late, they excelled despite the odds. The fact that they started late did not stack the odds against them; instead, it contributed to their success. So, do not be afraid to pull the plug on an unsuccessful endeavor and experiment with something new.

chapters.title: Age and experience are your advantages
chapters.text:
Frances Hesselbein became the CEO of the Girl Scouts of the United States in 1976 and headed the organization until 1990. She later went on to serve as the president of the Leader to Leader Institute. What’s particularly unique about Hesselbein’s story is that she didn’t have any formal leadership training, nor did her career follow the standard path to a C-level position. Her professional career didn’t even start until her mid-fifties. However, Hesselbein became one of the most esteemed CEOs of her time thanks to her openness to new ideas and the extensive breadth of her experience.

Hesselbein’s most quoted declaration is, “I never planned.” Her sole strategy was to engage in what seemed intriguing or necessary at the time without a predefined blueprint. She often emphasizes that keeping an open mind is essential for absorbing insights from new experiences.

We often assume our interests and aspirations, though evolving in the past, will remain static in the future — a phenomenon known as the “end of history illusion.”

Biologically, we do not stay the same, so our work and life preferences will change as we age. It is why career goals that once felt safe and certain can appear ridiculous when examined in the light of increased self-knowledge.

You are just as ever-changing as the various versions of yourself that have existed over time.

Research by University of Illinois psychologist Brent W. Roberts has shown predictable patterns in the evolution of personality traits over time. As we grow older, we generally become more conscientious, emotionally steady, affable, and less prone to neurosis.

Since personality evolves with experiences and varied situations, we cannot accurately set inflexible long-term objectives with limited knowledge and context exposure.

Significant personality transformations typically occur from our late teens to our late twenties, making early specialization a gamble on predicting the match quality for future versions of ourselves. While it's possible, the likelihood of success is uncertain.

So, David Epstein recommends that you shouldn’t plan-and-implement, but test-and-learn. Opt for quick, exploratory trials over elaborate, fixed plans. “Flirt with your possible selves,” he advises.

chapters.title: Being a generalist requires breadth and experimentation
chapters.text:
Italian sculptor Michelangelo created excellent works that are still admired by all today. Yet, contrary to popular belief, Michelangelo wasn’t inherently a prodigy as many historians portray him.

Art historian William Wallace describes Michelangelo as a master of the test-and-adapt approach. He frequently revised his sculptural designs mid-project and often left sculptures incomplete, shifting to more enticing endeavors.

Throughout his life, Michelangelo explored art in many forms. Not only was he an architect, engineer, painter, and sculptor, but he also temporarily set visual art aside to delve into poetry writing, half of which remained incomplete.

Like anyone searching for the optimum path for them, Michelangelo did not learn who he was or what he was carving from theory but in practice. He started with a concept, tested it, modified it, and willingly abandoned it for a more suitable project.

To become a generalist, you must be comfortable with experimentation.

Avoid having a narrow and myopic view of life; don't compare yourself to others, trying to follow in their footsteps. What worked for another person might not work for you.

Like Michelangelo viewed each marble block differently, embark on your journey and projects with an openness to change as you go. You must be willing to make a complete U-turn and abandon a previous goal should the need arise.

Investigations into creative individuals and leaders across areas from comics to tech innovation show that a collective of specialists can’t match the impact of individuals with broad expertise. Even if you transition away from a specific profession or field, the knowledge you've gained remains valuable.

When you push the boundaries, a lot of it is just probing. It has to be inefficient. ~ Arturo Casadevall

Assess yourself against your past, not against younger, more accomplished individuals who are different from you. Don’t let another's achievements rush your pace; everyone advances at their own speed. You may not know your destination yet, so feeling delayed serves no purpose. Don’t be afraid to try new things; it is the only way to learn who you are. Learn and adjust as you go.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Unlike the stories the popular media would have us believe about self-discovery and innovation, the journey to fulfillment is rarely orderly. It’s rarely like going from A to B. When examined in depth and over time, the stories usually get murkier.

If you examine the lives of highly successful people, you'll discover that there was a sampling period for most of them. There was a period when they tried different endeavors, learning about their abilities and interests before settling on a particular course. Successful individuals do not always have a focused head start.

What makes the Tiger path so appealing? It's because stories of child prodigies who specialized early and then went on to dominate their fields are attractive. Such an approach downplays the significance of side tracks, breadth, and experimentation in achieving success. It offers a streamlined, efficiency-driven formula with minimal uncertainty. Naturally, the appeal of a head start is undeniable. Yet, while experimentation doesn’t provide a simple, catchy recipe, it remains the more prevalent route to achieving success.

This finding doesn’t imply that specialization is inherently negative. At various points in our lives, we all engage in specialized activities. But, resist the temptation to buy into popular articles and keynote speeches promoting early intense specialization as a shortcut to avoid the uncertainties and explorations of diverse experiences.

Research has shown that head starts are overrated and that wandering intellectually and engaging in varied experiments are potent tools for success.

Try this



• Do you feel unaccomplished in your current endeavor, no matter how hard you try? This sensation might be because there's something about you that you're still yet to discover. Don't be afraid to sample different opportunities and see where the road leads.
• The breadth of your experience is essential to finding your match quality. Analyze one of your own.
